title: What to know before using Alnus glutinosa (European Alder)?
botany_desc: Alnus glutinosa, commonly known as the black alder or European alder, is a species of tree in the family Betulaceae. It is native to Europe and the eastern portion of Asia, ranging from Norway and Sweden in the north to Italy and Greece in the south, and as far east as Russia and Iran. The taxonomy of Alnus glutinosa is well-established, with no known subspecies or varieties. It is a member of the genus Alnus, which contains around 30 species of trees and shrubs, mostly found in the Northern Hemisphere.

Alnus glutinosa is a deciduous tree that can grow up to 30 meters (100 feet) tall, with a trunk diameter of up to 2 meters (6.5 feet). The tree has a broad, rounded crown and a straight, cylindrical trunk. The leaves are alternate, simple, and oval-shaped, with serrated edges and a pointed tip. They are dark green and shiny on the upper surface, and pale green and downy on the lower surface. The bark is smooth and grayish-brown when the tree is young, but it becomes rough and fissured with age. The tree produces small, conical flowers called catkins, which are wind-pollinated. The male catkins are yellowish-green and up to 10 centimeters (4 inches) long, while the female catkins are reddish-brown and up to 2 centimeters (0.8 inches) long.

Alnus glutinosa is also known by several other common names, including common alder, European black alder, and oval-leaved alder. There are no known variants of Alnus glutinosa that are recognized as distinct taxa. However, there is some variation in the shape and size of the leaves, as well as the length and color of the catkins, among different populations of the tree. These variations are not significant enough to warrant the recognition of subspecies or varieties.

Alnus glutinosa is found in a wide range of habitats, including wetlands, riverbanks, and forests. It is particularly common in areas with moist, well-drained soils, such as floodplains and alluvial forests. The tree is able to tolerate a wide range of soil types, including sand, clay, and loam, and it is also tolerant of acidic, neutral, and alkaline soils. Alnus glutinosa is native to Europe and Asia, but it has been introduced to other parts of the world, including North America, where it is now considered an invasive species in some areas.

Alnus glutinosa is a fast-growing tree that can live for up to 100 years. It reproduces sexually, through the pollination of its flowers and the subsequent development of seeds. The seeds are enclosed in small, winged fruits called samaras, which are dispersed by the wind. The tree also reproduces vegetatively, through the production of root suckers, which are new shoots that grow from the roots of mature trees. These root suckers can give rise to new trees, which are genetically identical to the parent tree. Alnus glutinosa is an important tree for wildlife, providing food and shelter for a variety of birds and mammals. It is also used for timber, fuel, and tannin production.
